segments.title: Axon License Plate Reader Contract — Data Ownership, Privacy, and Federal Access Protections
segments.summary: Joint Public Safety and Economic Development committee reviews proposed Axon contract for 26 license plate reader (LPR) cameras. Deputy Chief Trudeau explains LPR functionality: cameras read plates, data owned solely by SPD in a closed system (vendor has no access). LPRs used reactively for investigations, not live monitoring — vehicles involved in approximately 70% of crimes. Key discussion on federal immigration data access: SPD will not share LPR data with ICE or federal agencies absent a court order or subpoena. Chief confirms Axon system is opt-in by default (unlike previous Flock system that inadvertently left data-sharing enabled). Future goal: local law enforcement data-sharing MOAs with Onondaga County agencies, with explicit restrictions preventing further dissemination.

political_signal.description: Council members closely questioning LPR data privacy, federal immigration access protections, and data sharing safeguards before approving Axon contract

speakers.name: Jimmy Monto
speakers.role: Committee Co-Chair (Economic Development), Common Councilor District 5

speakers.name: Donna Moore
speakers.role: Committee Co-Chair (Public Safety), Common Councilor District 2

processing_notes: detected_departments included 'Department of Permits and Development' — false positive from 'development' in committee name; excluded
Speaker attribution: chair not explicitly identified; likely Monto (Econ Dev chair) since Moore is listed as 'joined by'
Previous LPR vendor referenced as having 'accidentally left a button on' sharing data — likely Flock Group (appears on every 2026 agenda)

segments.title: LPR Expansion Plans, Data Retention Policy, and Community Concerns on ICE-Connected Vendors
segments.summary: Continued discussion on Axon LPR contract. Council questions data breach liability (deferred to executive session). SPD plans to expand from 13 to 26 cameras at original proposed locations across all city quadrants, focusing on traffic convergence points. Community concern addressed about contracting with companies that also serve ICE — Chief argues restricting vendors by federal client list would severely limit SPD capability. Data retention: 30-day automatic deletion for unused bulk data; evidence extracted for investigations follows crime-specific retention per Criminal Procedure Law. Council member asks about cross-referencing LPR locations with Vision Zero data (no current correlation). Real-life example cited: north side homicide solved using LPR cameras. Meeting moves to executive session for contract terms.

political_signal.description: Council pressing on data breach liability, retention policies, geographic equity of camera placement, and ethical concerns about vendor relationships with federal immigration enforcement

speakers.name: Jimmy Monto
speakers.role: Committee Co-Chair (Economic Development), Common Councilor District 5
